subject: eddy-lazzarin

sectors_excited.name: Stablecoins & payments
sectors_excited.why: The pieces have finally come together — cheap, sub-second, global dollar transfers with real product-market fit. I spent much of this year on the unsolved UX (deterministic cross-chain payouts, x402-style flows); the bottlenecks are exactly where the next builders win. People come for the savings and stay for the network: custody, programmability, composability.

citations.quote: Payments has been one of the goals of crypto from the beginning. The pieces are finally coming together.
Everyone is talking about payments.

sectors_excited.name: Onchain privacy / confidential transactions
sectors_excited.why: I'll scream this forever: as crypto touches real finance, privacy stops being optional. Institutions treat it as table stakes; consumers take a functional level of privacy for granted until it's gone. The cryptography (Aztec/Noir, Railgun, Zcash-style shielding) is maturing — the UX isn't ready for normies yet, but the demand is inevitable.

citations.quote: Now I believe the lack of privacy onchain is one of the biggest sources of friction, if not a dealbreaker, for many institutions. It's critical.

sectors_excited.name: Zero-knowledge proofs & zkVMs
sectors_excited.why: Decades of theory crossing into practice at a staggering pace. Succinct verification is the substrate for scaling, privacy, and verifiable compute (incl. AI inference). The sumcheck/lookup approach we've pushed in Jolt is getting dramatically faster and simpler — a real public good for the whole stack.

citations.quote: Jolt just got 6x faster. And simpler. It's probably the fastest zkVM cycle-for-cycle by a wide margin.

sectors_excited.name: Private proof-of-personhood / digital identity
sectors_excited.why: AI has crushed the marginal cost of fake content, fake context, fake accounts. The defense is a credential that's cheap to get one of and nearly impossible to get two of — privacy and uniqueness as intrinsic properties. A root of trust (World ID is the strongest example) plus a stack of private, programmable credentials on top.

citations.quote: If we want to ensure tokens/rewards/credentials end up in the hands of real people (or even high-quality bots!) we need an ID system with *privacy* and *uniqueness* as intrinsic properties.

sectors_excited.name: Network tokens with real value accrual (fee switches, buyback-and-burn, 'closed loop')
sectors_excited.why: With regulatory conditions finally sane, network tokens can complete the economic loop: capture protocol revenue into the token and use issuance to fund growth. A protocol is a decentralized network-marketplace; the token is ownership in it. This is the king of token types and the design space I'm most excited to see mature.

citations.quote: We finally have the chance to build economically sustainable decentralized networks.
If the DAO agrees, Uniswap will finally become a "closed loop" network token: fees paid to the decentralized protocol will be captured by the token through a buyback and burn.

sectors_excited.name: AI × crypto (agent payments, x402, machines that own)
sectors_excited.why: Crypto is how machines can own — wallets, keys, and onchain payment rails let agents transact, pay for their own compute, and coordinate. x402 makes micropayments a viable business model online for the first time. The counterbalance to centralized AI also lives here.

citations.quote: x402 means micropayments might finally be a real business model online
crypto = how machines can own

sectors_excited.name: Fully onchain games
sectors_excited.why: Games push every envelope and are a serious incentive-design domain, not a hobby. Trustless game state unlocks composability — third parties extend, remix, and own pieces of a world no single party controls. Lower fees finally make the thousands-of-state-mutations interaction model affordable.
sectors_excited.age_note: thesis stated 2022-06; abiding interest, restated through 2025 via EVE Frontier playtests and ProofOfPlay — a long-horizon conviction, not a fresh trading view.

citations.quote: I believe fully on-chain games will fill a similar niche in coming years.

sectors_concerned.name: Memecoins / casino dynamics
sectors_concerned.why: Not technically interesting, and they actively set the industry back — making crypto look like a risky casino to the public, regulators, and would-be founders, and deterring great builders. Launches have cooled in late 2025 as policy clears the way for productive uses, which is healthy, but the reputational drag is real.

citations.quote: Memecoins alter how the public, regulators, and entrepreneurs see crypto.
I see the damage every day. You should too.

sectors_concerned.name: Excess leverage (perpetuals)
sectors_concerned.why: Perp dexes are highly engineered and have absorbed a lot of the speculative demand that used to chase memecoins — fine as products. But the system is now very effective at delivering cheap leverage, which can exacerbate crashes when market makers pull back. After the late-2025 drawdown, this is where I'd be most wary.

citations.quote: I think leverage is a great way to lose everything.

sectors_concerned.name: Company-backed tokens mislabeled as decentralized / pie-chart 'tokenomics'
sectors_concerned.why: Plenty of tokens described as network tokens (or memecoins) are really company-backed — value derives from a system one party controls, which means securities-like trust dependencies. A made-up pie chart isn't a protocol. Watch whether the value chain can actually operate without human control.

citations.quote: Some projects even describe their token as a Network Token or memecoin when it's really Company-backed.
A made up pie chart is not real work.

sectors_concerned.name: Vanity metrics (active addresses, TVL)
sectors_concerned.why: The headline usage numbers are easily gamed — one person can spin up endless addresses, and airdrop/reward farming is fully professionalized. We estimate real users at a small fraction of active addresses. Be skeptical of any narrative resting on these proxies.

citations.quote: It's extremely obvious why "active addresses" is flawed in multiple ways — yet no other measure seems to be better.

tokens_excited.name: UNI
tokens_excited.why: Excited about the design move to a closed-loop network token — protocol fees captured by the token via buyback-and-burn, with minting available to fund growth, made possible by a lot of legal work (the DUNI). This is admiration for the token mechanism, not a price call.

citations.quote: If the DAO agrees, Uniswap will finally become a "closed loop" network token: fees paid to the decentralized protocol will be captured by the token through a buyback and burn.

tokens_excited.name: ETH
tokens_excited.why: The canonical network token: value accrues via the EIP-1559 burn as blockspace is consumed, and activity is scaling onto L2s as fees collapse. Economically it's ownership in the marketplace that sells blockspace, not the commodity itself. A fundamentals/value-accrual view, not a price target.

citations.quote: I think ETH is already doing (fully automatic) buybacks via the EIP-1559 burn.
the token that captures value (via burn) from consumed block space

tokens_excited.name: BTC
tokens_excited.why: Found a comfortable product-market fit as 'digital gold' just as demand for a store of value rose. As the native asset of the most secure, decentralized network it has economic characteristics unlike any memecoin. Constructive on the fundamentals — not a price prediction.

citations.quote: being the native asset of what is arguably the most secure and decentralized network gives it economic characteristics (and potential, even if not yet realized) unlike any memecoin.

risk_regime.why: I don't trade sentiment or swing risk-on/risk-off — I read markets through the price-innovation cycle and trust fundamentals and instruments over the herd. Skeptical in analysis, optimistic in action: structurally constructive on building right now ('it's app time soon') while cautioning hard against leverage after the late-2025 drawdown. So: neutral as a trading posture, bullish on the technology.

citations.quote: the right balance is to be skeptical in analysis and optimistic in action
The winds of change are blowing across crypto. I think it's app time soon.

notes: I'm an engineer, not a fintech guy or a trader — these are fundamentals and token-design views, not price targets. The through-line for 2025 is industry maturation: stablecoins and payments wiring up, network tokens finally able to complete their economic loop, ZK and privacy moving from theory to table stakes, and proof-of-personhood becoming necessary as AI floods the internet. Despite all that, it's still early.
